title: Soy milk mordanting for cotton
subtitle: Using fermented soy milk to prepare cotton for natural dyes
excerpt: Soy milk mordanting is a non-toxic method for preparing cotton and other plant fibres to accept natural dyes. Soy protein coats the fibre, giving it a protein-like surface that binds dye more effectively. The technique requires three to four applications before dyeing.
sourceNotes: Soy milk mordanting for plant fibres is described in Yoshiko Wada et al., Shibori (1983, in copyright, substance only referenced) and in contemporary Japanese and British natural dye practice guides. The protein-coating mechanism is accepted in natural dye literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A substance that prepares fibre to accept and retain dye. Metallic salts are the standard mordants for wool; soy milk provides a protein coating that acts as a functional mordant for cotton and linen.

A fibre of animal origin (wool, silk, cashmere, alpaca) whose protein structure readily accepts many plant dyes with alum mordant. Cotton and linen are cellulose fibres that require different mordanting approaches.
A plant-based fibre (cotton, linen, hemp, bamboo) whose cellulose structure does not readily accept most natural dyes without a mordant that provides a bridging surface between fibre and dye.

content.text: that do not absorb most natural dyes as readily as
like wool. Soy milk mordanting gives the cotton a protein coating that makes it behave more like wool in the dye bath. The process is non-toxic, inexpensive, and suitable for use in a kitchen.

attrs.title: Dye-pot safety
attrs.body: Use pots dedicated to dye work. Keep them out of food preparation use after soy milk treatment.

content.text: Dilute the soy milk 1:1 with cold water in the dye-pot. Do not heat the bath; the soy proteins are denatured at high temperatures and lose their mordanting ability. A cold soak is the correct method.

Wet the cotton thoroughly in plain water first, then transfer it to the soy bath. Stir to ensure all surfaces are in contact with the liquid. Leave to soak for 1 hour at room temperature. Squeeze out the liquid but do not rinse; rinsing removes the soy coating you just applied.
Hang the cotton to dry completely in a warm room or outdoors. Once dry, repeat the soy bath soak and drying cycle. Three to four complete cycles produce enough protein coating for a useful
surface. The cotton will smell of soy milk; this disappears after dyeing and washing.

After the final drying, leave the cotton for at least 24 hours before dyeing. The protein coating bonds more firmly to the fibre when allowed to cure at room temperature. The cotton can be stored dry for weeks before dyeing if needed.

items.problem: The dyed cotton colour is still pale compared with wool dyed in the same bath
items.cause: Fewer than three soy applications, or the soy milk was diluted too far
items.fix: Apply at least three to four coats; each coat adds to the protein layer. Use the soy milk diluted only 1:1 with water, not more dilute.

items.problem: The soy milk has gone lumpy or sour
items.cause: Kept in a warm room for too long before use
items.fix: Fresh soy milk works best. Use within a day or two of opening, and keep refrigerated between applications. Slightly soured soy milk can still be used; it will smell worse but continues to work.
